coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive quantification of hydrogen-transmitting coenzyme in physiological pH using silver nanoparticles dispersed on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Shadjou, Nasrin
Saduooghi, Eissa
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: The good mechanical and biological performance of natural biopolymers is important factor in biomedical science. In metabolism, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is involved in redox reactions, carrying electrons from one reaction to another. On such a basis, much attention has been devoted to developing procedures to monitoring the presence of NAD at very low concentrations in a physiological environment and biological fluids. The good mechanical and biological performance of natural biopolymers is important factor in bone tissue engineering. Herein, a new platform based on silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) is developed for sensitive voltammetric detection of NAD in human plasma samples. N-GQDS-Ag NPs film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of GCE. Ag NPs were covalently bonded to N-GQDS films and used to bind of NAD biomarker. The novel detection scheme is a suitable electrochemical assay, which makes the NAD detection simple and rapid. The engineered electrochemical sensor showed a linear dynamic range and low limit of quantification limit of 1.45–7.9 μM and 1.45 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of NAD with high sensitivity and reproducibility. The prepared sensor detected NAD ion human plasma samples with high recovery values. We hope that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and point-of-care diagnosis of disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioanalysis
Hydrogen-transmitting coenzyme
Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical monitoring
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Uremia University and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by Uremia University and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Electrochemical deposition of N-GQDs on the surface of GCE. The cyclic voltammogram depicts grasignal growth of N-GQDs after 30 successive cycles in the range of −1 to +1 V with scan rate of 0.1 V/s. B) CVs for the electrochemical deposition of Ag NPs on the surface of N-GQDs/GCE. The Cycle No = 30 and the scan rate was 100 mV/s. The solution consisted of 1.5 mM aqueous solution of AgNO3 in HNO3, while rang of potential applied was −1 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature.

Typical CVs, LSVs, SWVs, and DPVs of GCE (A), N-GQDs-GCE (B), Ag NPs-GCE (C) and N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE (D), in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.1 M NAD. Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). SWVs parameters; step potential = 0.005, amplitude time = 0.02 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05. DPVs parameters; pulse amplitude: 50 mV, pulse width: 50 ms, pulse period: 0.2 s. CV and LSV parameters; sweep rate: 100 mV/s.
CVs of N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE in different scan in the presence of 0.1 M PBS.

A) CVs of N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE in the presence of 0.1 M NAD in different scan Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS. B) Dependency of peak current to the square root of sweep rates. C) Dependency of peak potential versus Napierian logarithm of scan rate.

A) SWVs of N-GQDS-Ag NPs-GCE in different concentrations of NAD in the standard with different concentrations (1.45, 1.49, 1.53, 1.58, 1.68, 1.73, 1.79, 1.85, 1.68, 1.73, 1.79, 1.85, 1.92, 1.99, 2.07, 2.15, 2.24, 2.34, 2.45, 2.57, 2.70, 2.84, 3.00, 3.18, 3.38, 3.61, 3.88, 4.18, 4.54, 4.96, 4.47, 6.09, 7.48, and 7.9 μM). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 10.0). B) Calibration curves of NAD using N-GQDS-Ag NPs-GCE. C) DPVs of N-GQDS-Ag NPs-GCE in different concentrations of NAD in the human plasma samples with different concentrations (0.817, 0.837, 0.857, 0.87, 0.90, 0.92, 0.95, 0.97, 1.00, 1.03, 1.06, 1.09, 1.13, 1.17, 1.21, 1.25, 1.30, 1.35, 1.40, 1.52, 1.59, 1.67, 1.75, 1.85, 1.95, 2.06, 2.19, 2.34, 2.51, 2.70, 2.93, 3.19, and 3.51 μM). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 10.0). D) Calibration curves of NAD using N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE in human plasma sample.
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of NAD in the standard samples using N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE.
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of NAD in the human plasma samples using N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE.
Inter-day precision, accuracy and recoveries of the method for assay of NAD.
Intra-day precision, accuracy and recoveries for determination of NAD.
Details of the stability of the proposed method in human plasma samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive quantification of hydrogen-transmitting coenzyme in physiological pH using silver nanoparticles dispersed on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Science, Uremia University, Uremia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 516560-65811, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The good mechanical and biological performance of natural biopolymers is important factor in biomedical science. In metabolism, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is involved in redox reactions, carrying electrons from one reaction to another. On such a basis, much attention has been devoted to developing procedures to monitoring the presence of NAD at very low concentrations in a physiological environment and biological fluids. The good mechanical and biological performance of natural biopolymers is important factor in bone tissue engineering. Herein, a new platform based on silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) is developed for sensitive voltammetric detection of NAD in human plasma samples. N-GQDS-Ag NPs film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of GCE. Ag NPs were covalently bonded to N-GQDS films and used to bind of NAD biomarker. The novel detection scheme is a suitable electrochemical assay, which makes the NAD detection simple and rapid. The engineered electrochemical sensor showed a linear dynamic range and low limit of quantification limit of 1.45–7.9 μM and 1.45 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of NAD with high sensitivity and reproducibility. The prepared sensor detected NAD ion human plasma samples with high recovery values. We hope that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and point-of-care diagnosis of disease.

ce:section.ce:para: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is a coenzyme found in all living cells. The compound is a dinucleotide, because it consists of two nucleotides joined through their phosphate groups. One nucleotide contains an adenine base and the other nicotinamide. Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide exists in two forms, an oxidized and reduced form abbreviated as NAD+ and NADH respectively [1].

In metabolism, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide is involved in redox reactions, carrying electrons from one reaction to another. The coenzyme is, therefore, found in two forms in cells: NAD+ is an oxidizing agent. It accepts electrons from other molecules and becomes reduced. This reaction forms NADH, which can then be used as a reducing agent to donate electrons. These electron transfer reactions are the main function of NAD [2]. However, it is also used in other cellular processes, the most notable one being a substrate of enzymes that add or remove chemical groups from proteins, in posttranslational modifications. Because of the importance of these functions, the enzymes involved in NAD metabolism are targets for drug discovery [3].

In organisms, NAD can be synthesized from simple building-blocks (de novo) from the amino acids tryptophan or aspartic acid. In an alternative fashion, more complex components of the coenzymes are taken up from food as the vitamin called niacin. Similar compounds are released by reactions that break down the structure of NAD [4]. These preformed components then pass through a salvage pathway that recycles them back into the active form. Some NAD is also converted into nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP); the chemistry of this related coenzyme is similar to that of NAD, but it has different roles in metabolism.

On such a basis, much attention has been devoted to developing procedures to detect the presence of NAD at very low concentrations in a physiological environment and biological fluids. One of interesting way is application of electrochemical methods based on nanomaterials. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibit impressive electrocatalytic activities [5]. N-GQDs were initially reported by cineaste researchers using a simple electrochemical approach [6]. Also, zero-dimensional N-GQDs are scarcely indicated as one electrocatalyst in fuel cells. The N-GQDs are favorably crystalling with a size of 2.5–8.5 nm of diameter and made up 5–7 layers of thickness [7,8].

After some of experimental study by our research group in this area [9–11], we found the intensity of peak current obtained by GQDs in electrochemical sensor is low. Also, stability of GQDs on the surface of electrode is small. Therefore, we need to use some of metals such as gold, iron, silver, and palladium to enhance the peak currents which is necessary for determination trace amount of analytes in real samples. So, in the present study, electrochemically synthesized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are used for enhancing the signal for electrochemical detection of NAD.

While the electrochemical synthesis of AgNPs is well established [12,13], the scheme presented herein enables particularly short deposition times, minimal solution preparation, and no requirement of an external linker or functionalizing ligand. Moreover, in-situ tailoring of the nanoparticle surface chemistry resulted in an improved affinity and selectivity. Therefore, the final nanocomposite was N-GQDs-AgNPs. This nanocomposite (N-GQDs-AgNPs) was used for electrochemical sensing of NAD using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and square wave voltammetry.

In this work, considering benefits of the AgNPs, and N-GQDs, we used these materials for the construction of a novel sensor to apply the synergy contributions on the enhancement of sensor characteristics. Considering that the electrochemical method possesses unique advantages for precise control over the redox reactions, we therefore hypothesize that, electrochemical deposition of N-GQDs on the surface of GCE may provide an alternative approach for tailoring the surface properties of thin films. On the other hand, these improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of AgNPs on the structure of N-GQDs. The proposed nanocomposite (N-GQDs-AgNPs) increases the contact area with the analyte (NAD), so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with NAD. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrochemistry, therefore modification of N-GQDs with AgNPs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. So, integration of AgNPs into N-GQDs structure can be providing an electrically conductive film towards increases Faradic currents. Excellent properties of N-GQDs and the advantages of AgNPs have received more attention for preparation of high performance electrode material. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the N-GQDs-AgNPs as a platform for the development of electrochemical sensor for detection of NAD. In summary, we investigated the fully electrochemical preparation of N-GQDs-AgNPs on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a novel strategy for interface science. The formation of N-GQDs-AgNPs was firstly monitored. The latent activity of N-GQDs-AgNPs coating towards detection and determination of NAD was then investigated. The redox behavior of prepared nano-sensor was then characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). Then, we used N-GQDs-AgNPs as a new interface for electro-oxidation and determination of NAD in human plasma samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the determination of NAD based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on N-GQDs-AgNPs films.

All chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. Silver Nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid, HNO3, NaOH, and NAD were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Additional dilute solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. The solutions were stable, and their concentrations did not change with time. Di-potassium phosphate and mono-potassium phosphate were obtained from Scharlau. Deionized water was purchased from Ghazi Pharmaceutical Company (Tabriz, Iran). Stock standard solution of NAD was prepared by dissolving an appropriate mass of NAD in PBS buffer (pH 7.4), and the daily standard working solutions of different concentrations were prepared by diluting the stock solution with buffer and were freshly used. Human blood samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a computer-controlled AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT 302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands), driven with NOVA 1.11 software. A conventional three-electrode cell was used with an Ag/AgCl (Methrom, The Netherlands) as a reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as a counter-electrode. The working electrode was GCE (d = 2 mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran).

TEM (transmission electron microscopy) analysis was recorded on a Philips CM30 electron microscope operated at 200 kV (Adelaide, Australia). The morphology of the prepared nanomaterials was evaluated with a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis which was conducted on FE-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The elemental analysis of the nanomaterials was recorded by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy of MIRA3 ESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The functional groups of the N-GQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra, Shimadzu model FT-IR prestige 21 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) using KBr discs. Crystallinity of the N-GQDs were tested using X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns by a Siemens D 5000 X-Ray diffractometer (Texas, USA) with a Cu Kα anode (λ = 1.54 Å) operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. The diffraction patterns were collected at room temperature from 3.0 to 10 and 10 to 70°. The dwell time was 12 s per increment and a step size of 0.05° per step was applied. Zeta potential values and the particle size distribution of N-GQDs samples were measured by Malvern particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Laser-scattering technique was used to determine the mean diameter and zeta-potential of N-GQDs by Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments (Malvern, UK) at room temperature. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of samples was recorded by a model Jade DSC, PerkinElmer (USA). Fluorescence imaging images were recorded on a Model Bh2-RFCA fluorescence Olympus microscope (Tokyo, Japan). A microplate reader of Awareness Technology (FL, USA) was applied to read absorbance of MTT assay samples at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm.

GQDs was synthesized and characterized according to our previous report [14]. An easy bottom-up method was used for the preparation of GQDs. At first, GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200 °C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution was added into the orange homogenous liquid dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month at 4 °C.

Briefly, 0.4 mL ethylenediamine and 1 g citric acid were added into 15 mL water and stirred to dissolve and mix for about 1 min. Afterwards, the mixture was placed into 20 mL teflon lined stainless hydrothermal synthesis autoclave and then the autoclave sealed and heated to 180 °C in oven and kept for 8 h. Finally, to remove any insoluble materials, the synthesized solution centrifuged 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained N-GQDs were stored at 4 °C for further uses [15].

The TEM studies were carried out to determine the actual size of the N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs. The TEM images obviously showed that the average particle sizes of the N-GQDs was estimated to be 10 nm, respectively. All of these results were confirmed by DLS analysis (Fig. S1A (see Supporting information)). Particle size of materials is normally observed by DLS (Fig. S1C (see Supporting information)). Similar results were obtained by AFM (Fig. S2 (see Supporting information)).

The XRD analysis was performed to confirm the structure of the N-GQDs from 3.0° to 80.0° (2θ) (Fig. S3 see Supporting information). The XRD patterns of N-GQDs show wide peak located at around 14 and 24°. The 24° with size of 0.375 nm is corresponded to an interlayer d-pacing where is longer than d-pacing of natural graphite. Similar results were obtained by UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectra (Figs. S4 and S5, respectively (see Supporting information)).

The procedure for electrodeposition of N-GQDs on GCE surface was not previously reported in the literature. Herein, the electrochemical deposition of N-GQDs on GCE is considered. In this work, the GCE was placed in the cell containing 5 mL of N-GQDs and subsequently, electrochemical deposition of N-GQDs on the electrode surface was conducted through CV with 30 consecutive cycles in the potential range of −1 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. 1A). For this purpose, N-GQDs modified GCE (N-GQDs/GCE) was prepared by CV technique. Fig. 1A shows the electrochemical deposition of N-GQDs on the surface of GCE.

Then, the AgNPs were electrochemically synthesized at the surface N-GQDs-GCE using CV technique. The electrochemical solution consisted of 1.5 mM aqueous solution of AgNO3 in HNO3, while rang of potential applied was −1 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature (Fig. 1B). Thus synthesized organic-inorganic nanohybride are referred as N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE throughout the text. After deposition, the electrodes were washed with distilled water and dried in air.

The surface morphology of GQDs, N-GQDs, N-GQDs-Ag NPs electrodeposited on the surface of GCE was analyzed by FE-SEM (Figs. S6 and S7, respectively (see Supporting information)). The FE-SEM images showed the sheet-like layer of graphene structure of N-GQDs. The investigation of the elemental analysis of the prepared QDs was determined by energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS) which shows in Fig. S8 (see Supporting information). Results confirmed that the main elements of N-GQDs are C, N and O. Also, for N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE, the main elements are Ag, C, N and O.

The voltammograms of bare GCE, N-GQDs-GCE, Ag NPs-GCE, and N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE (D) in the absence and presence of NAD were shown in Fig. 2 which the supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). Fig. 2A shows typical cyclic voltammograms of 0.1 M NAD in PBS using GCE. As can be seen, one anodic peak appeared in voltammogram, with potentials of 0.38 V using the GCE, at a potential sweep rate of 0.1 V/s. In the reverse sweep, however, no peaks appeared, indicating an irreversible heterogeneous electron transfer process for the oxidation of NAD. The peak current obtained using the N-GQDs-GCE, Ag NPs-GCE, and N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE electrode increased considerably with respect to that obtained using the bare GCE. As can be seen in Fig. 2B, in the absence of NAD, no peak appears at the N-GQDs-GCE. While NAD exhibits one redox peak, which proves that N-GQDs were coated on the surface of GCE. Importantly, the anodic peak potential for the oxidation of NAD at N-GQDs-GCE is about 0.3 V by CV and LSV. On the other hand, using DPV and SWV, the anodic peak potential reduce to 0.25 V. Interestingly, using Ag NPs-GCE, both oxidation peak current and potential were increased. These results confirmed that, Ag NPs were coated on the surface of GCE. Importantly, the stability of Ag NPs on the surface of GCE is low. Therefore we need to use combination of organic coater with Ag NPs. For this purpose, after modification of GCE by N-GQDs, Ag NPs were electrodeposited on the surface of N-GQDs-GCE. The data obtained clearly show that the combination of N-GQDs and Ag NPs definitely improve the characteristics of NAD oxidation (low oxidation potential and high peak current). That might be related to the excellent property of N-GQDs, such as high specific surface area and π-π interaction with GCE and facilitate electron transfer mechanism. On the other hand Ag NPs provide excellent electrical conductivity. Therefore, the role of Ag NPs was also important for promoting the electrochemical oxidation of NAD. So N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE could be substituted for the oxidation of NAD. Fig. 2D shows the voltammograms of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE in the absence and presence of 0.1 M NAD. The current response of NAD at the N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE is much higher than GCE, N-GQDs-GCE, Ag NPs-GCE which indicating that N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE possesses remarkable enhancement effects on the oxidation of NAD. It is noteworthy that the present response of NAD was dramatically improved when Ag NPs was introduced to the structure of N-GQDs-GCE. This can attribute to electrocatalytically nature of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE for the oxidation of NAD in physiological pH. This leads to a greater amount of NAD accumulation on the surface of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE. Therefore NAD could be more accumulated on the electrode surface due to their interaction with N-GQDs-Ag NPs.

A large peak current ratio of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE to N-GQDs-GCE and Ag NPs-GCE clearly indicates that the N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE is a good electrocatalyst for the oxidation of NAD, which can be due to synergetic effect of N-GQDs and Ag NPs on the electrooxidation of NAD. According to the obtained results, the intensity of peak currents obtained by N-GQDs-GCE is low. Therefore, we need to use some metals or metal nanoparticles such as silver which no requirement of an external linker or functionalization. In summary, Fig. 2D compares the CV, LSV, DPV and SWV responses of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE in the absence and presence of NAD. It is observed that a pair of redox peaks of the N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE was appeared, which confirmed electrodeposition of AgNPs on the surface of the N-GQDs. We suggest that the redox peaks correspond to the redox of the AgNPs on the surface of the pre-modified electrode.

In summary, the stepwise assembly of two materials on the GCE is accompanied by a change in the voltammetric response and a variation of the peak to peak separation between the cathodic and anodic waves (ΔE
P ) of the redox probe. An irreversible redox behavior of NAD was observed on the bare GCE (Fig. 2E) with a peak-to-peak separation of 0.2 V. Importantly, the response at the N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE was similar to the shape obtained on other modified electrode. In addition, the redox peak current increased and the ΔE
P decreased with Ag NPs assembly. We concluded that this is caused by the Ag NPs. First, NPs possess a concentration effect, a directional effect and a quantum size effect. They decrease the resistance of electron transfer and increase the electron transfer rate. Second, Ag NPs exhibit relatively good electron conductivity. Third, the high surface-to-volume ratio of the assembled NPs materials may greatly enhance the density of the sensor. These voltammograms showed that the AgNPs were successfully assembled on the N-GQDs and, as for the electron transfer, it was better than the N-GQDs-GCE. This may be ascribed to the electrostatic effect of NAD and AgNPs. Based on above discussed electrodes, N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE has greater interaction with NAD. Hence, higher peak current of NAD is expected because of existence of AgNPs on the structure of GQDs-α-CD. Therefore, the reason of this phenomenon is synergetic effect of N-GQDs, Ag NP, and catalytically species.

Scan rate is one of important items affecting electroactivity of various organic molecules. Fig. 3A presents typical CV of N-GQDs-Ag NPs-GCE in 0.1 M PBS solution at various potential sweep rates from 2 to 1000 mV/s. The peak's currents are proportional to sweep rates in the range of 2–500 mV/ s, pointing to the electrochemical activity of the surface redox couple. From the slope of this lines and using:
(1)
I p
=
n 2
F 2
4 RT
vA
Γ ∗
where Г ⁎ is the surface coverage of the redox species and ν being the potential sweep rate [16] and taking average of both cathodic and anodic results, Г ⁎ values of around 4.33 × 10−9 mol cm−2 have been derived.

Under different scan rates, electrochemical behavior of NAD was also studied, and the results were presented in Fig. 4 . As shown in inset Fig. 4, A linear relationship between the Epa vs. (Ag/AgCl) and lnv was obtained using the equation: Epa vs. (Ag/AgCl) = 0.039 lnv + 0.542 (R2 = 0.9856). For an adsorption controlled and totally irreversible electrode process, the relationship between Epa vs. (Ag/AgCl) and lnv is expressed through the Laviron equation [16]:
(2)
E p
=
RT
2 αnF
ln v + constant
where α, n, and ν are the electron-transfer coefficient, number of electron and potential sweep rate, respectively. Other symbols have their usual significance. The value of α
n can be easily calculated from the slope of Epa vs. (Ag/AgCl) vs. lnv. In this study, αn was calculated to be 0.53. Usually, α is assumed as 0.5 in a totally irreversible electrode process. The value obtained for n is 1.02 ≈ 1. Therefore, one electron is involved in the electrochemical oxidation of NAD.

The SWV is an effective and a rapid electroanalytical technique with lower concentration detection limits. The SWV was employed for the determination of NAD in the standard and human plasma samples. Fig. 5A displays the SWV response of the N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE for increasing NAD concentrations under the optimum experimental conditions. One well-defined oxidation peaks were observed. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship between the peak currents and the NAD concentration was obtained. The peak currents were proportional to NAD concentration in the range of 1.45–7.9 μM. The low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was estimated to be 1.45 μM for NAD. The low detection limit of the N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE could be ascribed to the high loading of AgNPs and N-GQDS by the present method and the rapid electron transfer between NAD and the N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE. The comparison of N-GQDs-AgNPs-GCE with other modified electrodes for NAD detection is shown in Table 1 . From Table 1 it can be seen that this method had the comparable sensitivity, detection limit and wide linear range for the detection with the simple electrode preparation procedure. Thus, this method allowed to sensitive determination of NAD.

For detection of NAD in human plasma sample of healthy person, different concentrations of NAD were applied by the standard addition method and obtained results are given in Table 2 . To test the accuracy of this electrochemical method, the results were compared with the standard method and summarized in Table 2. It was obvious that the NAD content detected by proposed method was lower than that detected by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry. It may be due to rather poor sensitivity and selectivity of ultraviolet-visible detection for NAD. Therefore, this method could be further developed for the clinical assay of intracorporeal NAD content.

Electrode stability related to the number of cycles was also checked. As obtained, with increase of the number of cycles to 100, the peak currents decrease very smoothly with error of 2.1% where in higher cycles is decreased remarkably, for example, in 100 cycles error percent is 10%. Results approve that the electrode is stable at least 100 replicate analysis.

FDA validation guidelines introduces the inter-day and intra-day evaluation methods to show the precision of the reported method where three concentrations (low, mid and high) of NAD were tested. For intra-day and inter-day precision, 4 replicated analyses of human plasma sample of NAD concentrations of 0.1, 0.3 and 0.8 μM were performed.

Tables 3 and 4
had shown the inter-day and intra-day precision, accuracy and recovery of the method in of human plasma samples. Concerning the maximum FDA permitted coefficient of variation value of 15%, the method has acceptable accuracy and precision.

These data revealed that the introduced method had an accepted level of precision. Recoveries varied from 89.2 to 103.7% by equating the calculated content with the nominal concentration of NAD. The experimental outcomes disclosed that the developed method was practical for the analysis of NAD in human plasma samples.

The aim of stability testing procedures is to clear the ways that whether the analyte is stable to environmental change including the matrix and storage conditions or not. Herein, we tested the short time temperature and freeze-thaw stability of the method to analysis of NAD in whole blood and plasma samples. As FDA recommended experimental procedure of short time temperature, we selected two different concentrations of NAD across the dynamic range and left the samples to stand at room temperature for at least 24 h before analysis. Results showed that the differences between the values measured in fresh samples and the prepared short time temperature samples were in the FDA allowed range. These results reflect that the NAD aliquots were stable at room temperature for at least 24 h. Also, freeze-thaw stability of the method were tested using whole blood and plasma aliquots containing NAD concentrations of 0.1 and 0.8 μM where the three FDA recommended freeze-thaw cycles were tested. As clear in Table 5 , the proposed analytical method possesses the allowed variations of lower 15% for whole plasma and whole blood samples.

We have successfully designed a new platform based on Ag NPs and N-GQDs for sensitive voltammetric detection of NAD in human plasma samples. N-GQDs-Ag NPs film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of GCE. Ag NPs were covalently bonded to N-GQDs films and used to bind of NAD biomarker. The novel detection scheme is a suitable electrochemical assay, which makes the NAD detection simple and rapid. The engineered electrochemical sensor showed a linear dynamic range and low limit of quantification limit of 1.45–7.9 μM and 1.45 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of NAD with high sensitivity and reproducibility. The prepared sensor detected NAD from human plasma samples with high recovery values. We conclude that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and point-of-care diagnosis of disease.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by Uremia University and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.09.019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The power to reduce: pyridine nucleotides-small molecules with a multitude of functions
